angle: WordPress Playground Blueprint API & Theme Tooling

findings.claim: The official Blueprint JSON schema is hosted at https://playground.wordpress.net/blueprint-schema.json and the source lives at github.com/WordPress/wordpress-playground/blob/trunk/packages/playground/blueprints/static/blueprint-schema.json

@wp-playground/client is the primary npm package for embedding and controlling WordPress Playground programmatically via iframe. It exposes startPlaygroundWeb() and client.runBlueprint() methods for programmatic theme installation.
@wp-playground/blueprints is a separate npm package containing TypeScript definitions and validation logic for the Blueprint JSON format, including step type interfaces like InstallThemeStep.
@wp-playground/cli provides command-line access to Playground with a --blueprint flag to initialize sites from JSON configuration files.

The installTheme blueprint step accepts a themeZipFile property (a Resource type) supporting these resource types: wordpress.org/themes (by slug), url (direct ZIP URL), vfs (virtual filesystem), github (repo + branch), and input (from blueprint parameters).

The writeFile and mkdir (createDirectory) blueprint steps can create block theme files directly in the virtual filesystem at /wp-content/themes/. This enables building a complete theme from scratch: style.css, theme.json, templates/index.html, etc.

The runPHP blueprint step can execute arbitrary PHP code within the WordPress context, enabling dynamic generation of theme.json with computed color palettes, typography settings, or other programmatic theme configuration.

The Blueprint schema defines these step types: login, defineSiteUrl, installPlugin, activatePlugin, installTheme, activateTheme, writeFile, createDirectory, moveFile, copyFile, removeFile, unzip, importFile, importWxr, runPHP, setPhpIni, and wp-cli.

WordPress Playground runs entirely in the browser via WebAssembly (PHP-WASM + SQLite). The PHP WASM binaries are approximately 15-30MB. Default PHP memory limit is 256MB. Storage is ephemeral by default unless Browser Storage (IndexedDB/OPFS) is enabled.
Blueprint JSON passed via URL parameters is limited by browser URL length (typically 2,000-8,000 characters). Complex blueprints must be hosted as JSON files and referenced by URL.

The Create Block Theme (CBT) plugin by Automattic's Theme Team has an internal REST API endpoint at wp-json/create-block-theme/v1/save that can sync Site Editor customizations back to theme files. Its core logic is in PHP classes Create_Block_Theme_Exporter and CBT_Theme_Generator.
The WordPress/blueprints GitHub repository serves as a community gallery of Blueprint examples, including examples for Create Block Theme setup and block theme testing.
An interactive Blueprint Builder tool exists at playground.wordpress.net/builder.html for writing and validating Blueprint JSON in real-time.

Carolina Nymark's Block Theme Generator at fullsiteediting.com/block-theme-generator/ is a standalone web tool that scaffolds starter block themes with No-Code, Basic, or Advanced foundations, often used in tandem with Playground for testing.

ZipWP (by Brainstorm Force/Astra) and InstaWP are commercial AI-powered tools that generate WordPress sites including theme styles from text prompts, using sandbox architectures similar to Playground.

Outbound requests from Playground require CORS-enabled endpoints and HTTPS only. Standard PHP socket functions are unavailable; wp_safe_remote_get calls are translated to JavaScript fetch() via a translation layer.
Large theme ZIPs (over 30-50MB) significantly impact Playground boot times because they must be unzipped into the virtual file system on every page load.

The WordPress Community Themes project (github.com/WordPress/community-themes) uses WordPress Playground to provide a pre-configured environment where anyone can build a theme visually and submit contributions.

Themes can be installed from GitHub repositories using the installTheme step with resource type 'github', specifying repository and branch. This requires the 'networking' feature to be enabled in the blueprint.

The Playground client API flow is: startPlaygroundWeb(options) to initialize, client.isReady() promise to wait for boot, then client.runBlueprint(blueprint) to execute steps. A blueprint can also be passed directly into the startPlaygroundWeb options.

key_insights: WordPress Playground's Blueprint API provides a complete programmatic pipeline for theme generation: writeFile/mkdir steps can create theme files from scratch, runPHP can dynamically generate theme.json, and installTheme can load pre-built zips -- all controllable from JavaScript via @wp-playground/client's startPlaygroundWeb() and runBlueprint() methods.

The Create Block Theme plugin's internal REST API (POST /wp-json/create-block-theme/v1/save) and PHP classes (CBT_Theme_Generator, Create_Block_Theme_Exporter) can be triggered programmatically via runPHP or wp-cli blueprint steps, enabling a pipeline where visual Site Editor customizations are automatically exported as theme files.

The competitive landscape for AI-powered theme generation on Playground is nascent -- ZipWP and InstaWP use similar sandbox architectures but are full SaaS platforms, while no existing tool specifically combines LLM-generated theme.json/templates with Playground's Blueprint API for instant in-browser preview. This is a clear gap our Block Theme Generator can fill.
gaps: Exact current npm version numbers for @wp-playground packages could not be verified with high confidence -- search results returned conflicting version numbers (0.16.x vs 3.1.x), likely due to a major version bump or monorepo versioning changes
No documentation found on maximum writeFile content size limits within blueprints -- unclear if there is a practical ceiling for inline theme file content in blueprint JSON

The Create Block Theme REST API endpoint structure is not fully documented publicly -- the wp-json/create-block-theme/v1/save endpoint was mentioned but full API schema with all available endpoints and parameters is not in official docs
Performance benchmarks for Playground with custom themes (boot time with writeFile-created themes vs installTheme from zip) are not published anywhere
No examples found of anyone combining LLM-generated block theme code with WordPress Playground blueprints in a production tool -- this appears to be genuinely novel territory
